---
title: "2001: A Quarto Odyssey"
subtitle: "I'm sorry Dave, Word can't do that"
author: "Tiago Jacinto"
institute: "FMUP · RISE-Health"
date: 2026-07-03
date-format: "D MMMM YYYY"
lang: en
format:
  fmup-revealjs:
    footer: "2001: A Quarto Odyssey · FMUP/MEDCIDS Summer School 2026"
    slide-number: c/t
    chalkboard: false
    incremental: false
---

## Agenda

- The problem: three tools that don't talk to each other
- Part I — Zotero + Better BibTeX
- Part II — Quarto + R
- Part III — Writing and export
- FAIR principles
- Next steps

## Before we start: what to install

Install these on your own computer before the course:

- **R** ([cloud.r-project.org](https://cloud.r-project.org)) + **RStudio** ([posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop](https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/)).
- **Quarto** — bundled with recent RStudio; otherwise [quarto.org/docs/get-started](https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/).
- **Zotero 7+** ([zotero.org/download](https://www.zotero.org/download/)) + **Better BibTeX**.

New or rusty in RStudio? See [tiagojct.eu/rsbp](https://tiagojct.eu/rsbp/).

A **GitHub account** is optional — only needed for cloning and version control.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Zotero is a desktop app

Zotero runs on your computer, not in the browser. Install it now if you don't have it yet, plus the [browser connector](https://www.zotero.org/download/connectors).
:::

## Get the materials

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
![](images/repo-qr.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
::: {.column width="55%"}
[github.com/tiagojct/quarto-odyssey-lab](https://github.com/tiagojct/quarto-odyssey-lab)

1. Clone `git clone https://github.com/tiagojct/quarto-odyssey-lab.git`, or **Code → Download ZIP**, or **Use this template**.
2. Open the project in **RStudio** (double-click `quarto-odyssey.Rproj`).
3. Open `course/course.qmd` and click **Render** (or `quarto preview course/course.qmd` in the RStudio Terminal).

[No local install? The repo ships a devcontainer — open in GitHub Codespaces as a fallback.]{.dimmed}
:::
::::

## The problem

A health researcher does three things repeatedly: **manages references**, **analyses data**, **writes**.

In most cases, across three separate tools that don't talk to each other.

Zotero in one corner, RStudio in another, Word in a third.

## The result is predictable

- Duplicated files
- Outdated references
- Tables copied by hand from one place to another
- Formatting redone every time the document changes destination

[The typical number of different versions of a manuscript in circulation during peer review: **5 to 8**.]{.column-margin}

## Word vs Quarto

| Scenario | Word | Quarto |
|---|---|---|
| Change a data point | Redo the table by hand | Re-renders on its own |
| Change the citation style | 1–3 hours | One line in the YAML, seconds |
| Submit in another format | Reformat everything | `format: [docx, pdf, html]` |
| Review what changed | Compare visually | `git diff` — plain text |

## What is Quarto

[Quarto](https://quarto.org) — *open source* scientific publishing system.

It brings together three things that usually live apart:

- Text in **Markdown**
- **R** code (or Python, Julia) that runs when you render
- **Citations** managed by Zotero

One `.qmd` file → `.docx`, PDF, or HTML, your choice.

## What you'll be able to do by the end of the day

- Set up Zotero + Better BibTeX for stable *cite keys*
- Add references in five different ways
- Check for retractions with Retraction Watch
- Write a `.qmd` with YAML, Markdown and R code
- Produce tables and figures that update with the data
- Export to `.docx`, PDF and HTML — and switch style in seconds

# Part I — Zotero + Better BibTeX {.center}

## What is Zotero

[Zotero](https://www.zotero.org) — reference manager. *Open source*, free, *desktop* application + *online* sync.

It stores three things per reference:

- **Metadata** (authors, title, DOI)
- **Attachments** (the PDF)
- **Annotations** (highlights, notes)

It integrates directly with Quarto — no copy and paste.

## Five ways to add references

1. Browser connector
2. DOI or PMID (*magic wand*)
3. *Drag-and-drop* of a PDF
4. Manual entry
5. `.bib`/`.ris` import

## Better BibTeX

Stable *cite keys* (`silva2024asthma`, not a random code) and automatic export of the library to `.bib`.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Non-negotiable rule

Always check whether an article has been **retracted** before citing it. Zotero shows a red warning; Retraction Watch maintains the database.
:::

# Part II — Quarto + R {.center}

## One project, simple folders

```
my-article/
├── manuscript.qmd
├── references.bib
├── data/
│   └── cohort.csv
└── output/
    ├── manuscript.docx
    ├── manuscript.pdf
    └── manuscript.html
```

**Relative** paths, always. `data/cohort.csv`, never `C:\Users\...`.

## R code inside the document

````markdown
```{{r}}
#| label: tbl-demographics
#| tbl-cap: "Sample characteristics"

cohort |>
  group_by(sex) |>
  summarise(n = n(), idade = mean(age)) |>
  gt()
```
````

The table updates on its own whenever `cohort.csv` changes.

## Tables and figures, automatic cross-references

- `knitr::kable()` or `gt()` for tables
- `ggplot2` for figures
- `@tbl-demographics`, `@fig-fev1-age` — references in the text that update on their own if you reorder the document

# Part III — Writing and export {.center}

## Citations in seconds

```yaml
bibliography: references.bib
csl: vancouver.csl
```

`@silva2024asthma` → Silva (2024). Change `csl: nature.csl`, re-render — **the whole** bibliography changes style, without touching the text.

## One file, three formats

```yaml
format:
  html: default
  docx: default
  pdf:
    documentclass: scrartcl
```

`.docx` to collaborate with co-authors, PDF to archive, HTML to share — all from the same `manuscript.qmd`.

## FAIR principles

**F**indable · **A**ccessible · **I**nteroperable · **R**eusable

A well-organised Quarto project — data, code, text kept separate, everything in plain text, everything in Git — meets these principles almost by construction.

## Next steps

- Version control with **Git**
- **`renv`** to pin R package versions
- **Positron**, the successor to RStudio
- Journal templates and Quarto extensions

# Why "2001: A Quarto Odyssey"? {.center}

## A reference, not a requirement

The name is a pun on *2001: A Space Odyssey* (Kubrick, 1968) — the onboard computer **HAL 9000**, which fails and complicates the mission.

The subtitle rewrites the film's most famous line: *"I'm sorry, Dave. I'm afraid I can't do that."*

It's what Word tells you, silently, every time you try to do something reproducible inside it.

You don't need to have seen the film. But after today, it might be worth it.

## Let's get started

Open `course/course.qmd` in RStudio — it's today's script, and it stays with you afterwards.

[github.com/tiagojct/quarto-odyssey-lab](https://github.com/tiagojct/quarto-odyssey-lab)

Tiago Jacinto · FMUP / RISE-Health
